q: What are the six Microsoft Responsible AI principles, and what does each one mean in plain terms?
a: Microsoft defines six Responsible AI principles: Fairness means AI systems should treat all people equitably without bias based on gender, race, or other protected characteristics. Reliability and Safety means AI must perform consistently and avoid unsafe outcomes even in edge cases. Privacy and Security means AI systems must protect personal data and resist adversarial attacks. Inclusiveness means AI should be designed to work for people of all abilities, backgrounds, and circumstances. Transparency means AI behavior should be understandable and explainable to the people who use and are affected by it. Accountability means humans must remain responsible for AI decisions, with governance processes in place to review and correct AI behavior. These six principles are tested heavily on AI-900 and appear as scenario matching questions — given a situation, identify which principle is being violated or upheld.
fj: Responsible AI is mostly about preventing bias — fairness is the most important principle, and the others are secondary.
fs: All six principles are co-equal and independently testable on AI-900. Omitting any one creates a blind spot: a system can be perfectly fair yet unsafe if it fails under unusual conditions (Reliability), or fair yet unaccountable if no human can override its decisions. Microsoft tests all six with equal frequency.

q: What is the difference between fairness and inclusiveness as Responsible AI principles, and give a concrete example of each?
a: Fairness is about ensuring AI treats similar people similarly — the system should not make biased predictions based on protected attributes like race, gender, or age. A classic failure: a loan-approval model that denies qualified applicants because the training data historically under-served a demographic group. Inclusiveness is about ensuring AI is accessible and useful to people with disabilities or from diverse backgrounds — the system should not exclude users who have hearing impairments, speak minority languages, or lack broadband access. A classic failure: a voice assistant that only works reliably for native English speakers with standard accents. Both are Responsible AI principles, but they address different axes of harm: fairness is about equitable treatment in outcomes, inclusiveness is about equitable access to the system itself.
fj: Fairness and inclusiveness are the same principle — treating everyone equally covers both.
fs: They address distinct concerns. Fairness targets decision bias in outputs (did the model discriminate?). Inclusiveness targets accessibility in design (can everyone use the interface?). A system can be perfectly fair in its decisions yet exclude users who rely on screen readers because the UI was never tested with assistive technology. Exam questions often use scenarios to distinguish them.

q: Match each AI workload type to its correct definition: computer vision, natural language processing, document processing, generative AI.
a: Computer vision workloads involve extracting meaning from images and video — tasks include image classification (labeling what is in a photo), object detection (locating and labeling multiple items in an image), optical character recognition (reading text from images), and facial detection. Natural language processing (NLP) workloads involve understanding and generating human language in text or speech form — tasks include sentiment analysis, entity recognition, translation, and speech-to-text. Document processing workloads extract structured information from semi-structured documents like invoices, forms, and receipts using a combination of OCR and field extraction models. Generative AI workloads produce new content such as text, images, code, or audio in response to prompts — examples include summarization, question answering, and image generation. All four categories are tested on AI-900 in scenario-matching questions: given a business requirement, identify the right workload type.
fj: Document processing is just OCR — any service that reads text from a document qualifies as document processing.
fs: OCR extracts raw text from images. Document processing goes further: it identifies structured fields (invoice number, date, line-item amounts) using trained field extraction models. Azure AI Document Intelligence is the dedicated document processing service; plain OCR from Azure AI Vision is a lower-level building block within it.

q: What are the key differences between regression, classification, and clustering as machine learning techniques, and when would you use each?
a: Regression predicts a continuous numeric value from input features. Use it when the output is a quantity — predicting house prices from size and location, or forecasting energy demand from weather data. The model learns a function mapping inputs to a number on a continuous scale. Classification predicts which discrete category an input belongs to. Use it when the output is a label — spam vs. not-spam email, fraud vs. legitimate transaction, or diabetic vs. healthy patient. Binary classification has two labels; multi-class has more. Clustering groups data points by similarity without predefined labels, making it unsupervised learning. Use it when you have no labeled data and want to discover structure — segmenting customers by purchasing behavior, or grouping news articles by topic. On AI-900 exam questions typically describe a business scenario and ask you to identify the correct ML technique.
fj: Clustering is a type of classification because both organize data into groups.
fs: Classification requires labeled training data and predicts which known class a new example belongs to — it is supervised. Clustering has no labels and discovers unknown groupings from raw data — it is unsupervised. You cannot cluster your way to a fraud-detection model because clustering does not know what fraud looks like; you need labeled examples and classification.

q: What is deep learning, and how does it differ from traditional machine learning?
a: Deep learning is a subset of machine learning that uses artificial neural networks with multiple hidden layers to learn hierarchical representations directly from raw data. Traditional ML requires humans to engineer features from data (e.g., extracting edge angles from an image before passing them to a classifier). Deep learning networks learn these intermediate representations automatically during training by adjusting millions of weights through backpropagation. This makes deep learning powerful for unstructured data: images, audio, and text where the relevant features are not obvious upfront. The trade-off is that deep learning typically needs much more labeled training data and GPU compute than classical ML algorithms like decision trees or linear regression. On AI-900 you need to know: deep learning uses neural networks with many layers, excels at images/speech/text, and underlies modern NLP models including the Transformer architecture.
fj: Deep learning is just a more powerful version of traditional ML that always performs better, regardless of how much data you have.
fs: For structured tabular data with limited samples, classical ML algorithms like gradient-boosted trees often outperform neural networks with far less compute. Deep learning's advantage is on unstructured data at scale. On AI-900 you are not expected to know when to prefer one algorithmically — just that deep learning requires more data and compute, and enables the capabilities that power modern AI services.

q: At the AI-900 level, what do you need to know about the Transformer architecture and why it matters for modern AI?
a: The Transformer is the neural network architecture that powers modern large language models (LLMs) and many vision models. Its defining innovation is the self-attention mechanism: instead of processing tokens sequentially (as older RNNs did), Transformers compute relationships between all tokens in a sequence simultaneously, enabling them to model long-range dependencies efficiently. Transformers come in three structural forms: encoder-only models (like BERT) that build rich representations of text for tasks like classification and entity recognition; decoder-only models (like GPT) that generate text autoregressively token by token; and encoder-decoder models that transform one sequence into another, ideal for translation and summarization. For AI-900 you do not need the math — you need to know: (1) Transformers use attention to weigh the importance of all words in context simultaneously, (2) they enable both understanding and generation of language at scale, (3) they are the architecture underneath Azure OpenAI Service's GPT and DALL-E models.
fj: Transformers are a type of recurrent neural network (RNN) that processes text word by word from left to right.
fs: RNNs process sequences step by step, suffering from vanishing gradients with long sequences. Transformers replaced RNNs precisely by eliminating sequential processing — the self-attention mechanism looks at every token simultaneously, allowing the network to capture long-range dependencies without forgetting earlier context. This is why Transformers scale to billion-parameter models where RNNs could not.

q: In Azure Machine Learning, what is the purpose of training data vs. validation data, and what is overfitting?
a: Training data is the labeled dataset the model learns from — it adjusts its parameters to minimize prediction error on these examples. Validation data is a held-out subset (typically 20% of the labeled dataset) that the model never trains on; it is used during training to measure how well the model generalizes to unseen examples and to tune hyperparameters. The distinction matters because a model can memorize the training set without learning genuine patterns — this is called overfitting. An overfit model achieves low error on training data but high error on validation data, meaning it will fail on real-world inputs. Signs of overfitting: training accuracy is much higher than validation accuracy. Remedies include regularization, more training data, or a simpler model. On AI-900 you need to know: labels are the correct answers in the training data; features are the input variables; and validation data tells you whether the model is generalizing.
fj: You evaluate a model's quality by checking its performance on the training data — if training accuracy is high, the model is ready to deploy.
fs: Training accuracy is unreliable as a deployment signal because a model can achieve perfect training accuracy by memorizing the data without learning patterns that generalize. You must evaluate on a held-out validation set (data the model never saw during training) to get an honest estimate of real-world performance. Azure ML's automated ML reports both training and validation metrics for exactly this reason.

q: What is Azure Machine Learning's Automated ML (AutoML) feature, and what does it automate?
a: Azure Machine Learning Automated ML (AutoML) automates the model selection and hyperparameter tuning steps of the machine learning workflow. You provide a labeled training dataset and specify the task type (classification, regression, or time-series forecasting); AutoML then tries many different algorithm and hyperparameter combinations, trains each one, evaluates it on a validation set, and returns the best-performing model. This removes the need for a data scientist to manually try dozens of algorithms. AutoML also handles basic data preprocessing steps such as missing value imputation and one-hot encoding. In the Azure ML studio UI, AutoML experiments are configured as a wizard with no code required. The trade-off: AutoML finds a good model, not necessarily the optimal one, and the resulting model may be harder to explain than a manually tuned simple model. On AI-900 you need to know AutoML: (1) automates algorithm and hyperparameter selection, (2) requires labeled data, and (3) is accessible via the Azure ML studio UI with no coding.
fj: AutoML in Azure Machine Learning generates the training data automatically by scraping it from the web, so you do not need a labeled dataset.
fs: AutoML automates the modeling process (algorithm selection, hyperparameter tuning), not the data collection process. You must supply a labeled training dataset. AutoML does nothing to help you acquire or annotate the data; its job starts after the data exists and the ML task is defined.

q: What are the key data and compute services in Azure Machine Learning, and when do you use each?
a: Azure Machine Learning has two primary compute types: compute instances and compute clusters. A compute instance is a managed virtual machine (similar to a cloud-based laptop) used for interactive development in Jupyter notebooks and for running AutoML experiments in the studio. A compute cluster is a scalable pool of VMs that scales to zero when idle, used for training jobs, pipeline steps, and batch inferencing. On the data side, Azure ML datastores are connections to Azure storage (Blob, Data Lake, SQL) that let you reference data without embedding credentials in code; datasets (or data assets in v2) are versioned, tracked snapshots of data used in experiments. For model management and deployment, Azure ML registers trained models in a model registry for versioning, and deploys them as online endpoints (real-time REST API) or batch endpoints (asynchronous scored jobs). On AI-900 you just need to know these service names and their purposes — not their SDK syntax.
fj: Azure Machine Learning is primarily a data storage service, similar to Azure Blob Storage, with some ML features added on.
fs: Azure ML is a managed MLOps platform — it handles experiment tracking, compute management, model versioning, and deployment, but it uses Azure Blob Storage and Azure Data Lake as underlying storage; it does not replace them. The exam tests your ability to identify which Azure ML component serves which purpose in the ML workflow.

q: What is image classification, and how does it differ from object detection?
a: Image classification assigns a single label to an entire image — the model answers 'what is the main subject of this photo?' For example, classifying a photo as 'cat' or 'dog.' The output is a label and a confidence score for the whole image; there is no information about where in the image the subject appears. Object detection goes further: it identifies multiple objects in the same image and draws a bounding box around each one with its label and confidence score. For example, detecting 'person' at coordinates (x1,y1)-(x2,y2) and 'car' at different coordinates in the same street photo. Object detection is used for autonomous driving, security cameras, and retail shelf analysis. The key distinction: classification answers 'what category?', detection answers 'what category, and where in the image?' Both capabilities are available in Azure AI Vision; Custom Vision (now part of Azure AI Vision) lets you train your own classifiers and object detectors on custom datasets.
fj: Object detection and image classification are the same capability — both tell you what objects are in an image.
fs: Classification gives one label per image with no spatial information. Object detection gives multiple labels, each with a bounding box showing where in the image the object is located. This spatial information is essential for use cases like counting cars in a parking lot or detecting safety equipment on construction workers — tasks where you need to know not just what is present but where.

q: What is optical character recognition (OCR) and what Azure service provides it?
a: Optical character recognition (OCR) is the process of detecting and extracting text from images. This includes printed text on signs, product labels, and scanned documents, as well as handwritten text. OCR converts visual text content into machine-readable strings that can be indexed, searched, or processed programmatically. In Azure, OCR is provided by the Azure AI Vision service (specifically the Read API within it). Azure AI Vision OCR supports many languages including Latin, Cyrillic, Arabic, Devanagari, Chinese, Japanese, and Korean scripts. For documents with structured fields (invoices, forms, receipts), Azure AI Document Intelligence builds on top of OCR to also identify field names and values, giving you structured key-value pairs rather than raw text. On AI-900 you need to know: OCR = reading text from images = Azure AI Vision; structured field extraction from documents = Azure AI Document Intelligence.
fj: Azure AI Document Intelligence is just a more powerful version of OCR that you use instead of Azure AI Vision for all text extraction needs.
fs: Azure AI Vision OCR is the right tool for reading raw text from natural scene images (signs, labels, whiteboard photos). Azure AI Document Intelligence is for extracting structured information from business forms and documents — it knows the meaning of fields, not just the text. Choosing between them depends on whether you need raw text or structured key-value data.

q: What is the difference between facial detection and facial recognition, and why does the distinction matter for Responsible AI?
a: Facial detection identifies the presence and location of human faces in an image — it draws bounding boxes around faces and can infer attributes such as approximate age, emotion, head pose, and presence of accessories like glasses. Facial detection does not identify who a specific person is. Facial recognition goes further: it matches a detected face against a database of known individuals to determine their identity. Azure AI Face service provides facial detection and limited attribute analysis. Full facial recognition (matching to identify specific people) in Azure requires additional approval and is subject to Microsoft's Responsible AI standards for Face because of the significant privacy risks of mass surveillance. This distinction matters because facial recognition enables tracking individuals without their consent, raising serious fairness, privacy, and accountability concerns. On AI-900 you need to know: the Face service detects faces and their attributes; recognition is a gated capability requiring justification.
fj: Azure AI Face service can identify any person in a photo by matching them against a database, and this is available to all customers by default.
fs: Facial identification against a database of known individuals is access-gated by Microsoft because of its potential for misuse. Standard access to the Face service provides detection, attribute estimation, and face verification (is this the same person as in photo A?), but not open-ended identification from an arbitrary database. This is one of the most commonly misunderstood distinctions on the AI-900 exam.

q: What capabilities does Azure AI Vision provide, and how does it differ from Azure AI Face?
a: Azure AI Vision is a broad computer vision service that includes: image analysis (generating captions, tagging objects and scenes, detecting people, recognizing brands and landmarks), optical character recognition (reading printed and handwritten text via the Read API), spatial analysis (counting people and detecting movement in video streams), and background removal. Azure AI Face is a narrower service focused specifically on faces: it detects faces in images, estimates facial attributes (age estimate, head pose, whether the person is wearing glasses), verifies whether two photos show the same person (face verification), and can find similar faces in a collection. The key boundary: Azure AI Vision can detect whether faces are present as part of broader image analysis, but for detailed facial attribute analysis or face matching you use the Azure AI Face service. On AI-900 exam questions typically ask you to match a scenario (e.g., 'count customers entering a store') to the correct service.
fj: Azure AI Vision and Azure AI Face are the same service — Azure AI Vision handles everything related to images including all face analysis.
fs: They are separate Azure AI services with different API endpoints and pricing. Azure AI Vision is a general-purpose vision service; Azure AI Face is a specialized service for face-specific operations. The exam tests whether you can select the correct service for a given scenario — a company wanting to verify that a job applicant matches their ID photo would use Face (face verification), not the general Vision service.

q: What is key phrase extraction in NLP, and what Azure service provides it?
a: Key phrase extraction is an NLP technique that automatically identifies the main concepts and topics in a block of text, returning them as a list of short phrases. For example, given a customer review saying 'The hotel had excellent room service but the check-in process was slow and the parking was very expensive,' key phrase extraction would return phrases like 'excellent room service,' 'check-in process,' and 'parking.' This is useful for summarizing large volumes of text, tagging content for search, and quickly understanding what topics appear in unstructured feedback. Key phrase extraction is a preconfigured (prebuilt) feature — you do not need to train a model. In Azure it is available through the Azure AI Language service. On AI-900 you need to know: key phrase extraction = identify main concepts in text = Azure AI Language service; it is preconfigured and requires no training.
fj: Key phrase extraction and text summarization are the same capability — both reduce long text to its most important parts.
fs: Key phrase extraction returns a flat list of important noun phrases found in the text, with no prose output. Text summarization generates a condensed readable paragraph that captures the meaning of the original. They are different features in Azure AI Language: key phrase extraction is for topic discovery and tagging; abstractive summarization is for producing readable condensed versions of long documents.

q: What is named entity recognition (NER), and how is it different from key phrase extraction?
a: Named entity recognition (NER) identifies and classifies specific real-world entities mentioned in text into predefined categories: people (Person), places (Location), organizations (Organization), dates and times (DateTime), monetary values (Money), and other types. For example, in the sentence 'Microsoft announced the Azure AI Foundry update in Seattle on June 5, 2025,' NER would tag 'Microsoft' as Organization, 'Azure AI Foundry' as a product, 'Seattle' as Location, and 'June 5, 2025' as DateTime. Key phrase extraction identifies the most important phrases in text without categorizing them — it tells you what is being discussed, not what type of thing each phrase represents. NER is used for information extraction tasks like building knowledge graphs, populating databases from documents, and compliance (detecting PII). Both are preconfigured features in Azure AI Language, requiring no model training for standard entity types.
fj: NER is just a fancier version of key phrase extraction that also labels the phrases it finds.
fs: Key phrase extraction finds the most salient phrases in text without any type classification — it does not know whether 'Microsoft' is a company or a person. NER goes further by assigning each recognized entity a specific category. This difference matters in practice: a support ticket routing system needs NER to extract 'customer name' (Person) and 'product' (Organization/Product) as structured fields, not a flat list of important phrases.

q: What is sentiment analysis in Azure AI Language, and what output does it produce?
a: Sentiment analysis determines the emotional tone of a piece of text — whether it is positive, negative, neutral, or mixed. Azure AI Language's sentiment analysis feature returns a document-level sentiment label (positive/negative/neutral/mixed) along with confidence scores for each sentiment class. It also supports opinion mining (sometimes called aspect-based sentiment analysis), which links sentiments to specific aspects mentioned in the text — for example, 'The battery life is great (positive) but the screen is dim (negative)' would surface two aspect-sentiment pairs rather than a single mixed label. Sentiment analysis is a preconfigured feature requiring no model training. Common use cases: analyzing customer reviews at scale, monitoring brand sentiment on social media, and routing support tickets by urgency. The service is part of Azure AI Language. On AI-900 you must know that opinion mining is a feature of sentiment analysis, not a separate service.
fj: Sentiment analysis only tells you whether text is positive or negative — it gives one label for the entire document and nothing more.
fs: Azure AI Language's sentiment analysis also supports opinion mining, which breaks down the sentiment by entity or aspect mentioned in the text. This allows nuanced analysis: a single review can be positive about the food, negative about the service, and neutral about the ambiance, all extracted as structured aspect-sentiment pairs. The exam may test this distinction.

q: What is the difference between the Azure AI Language service and the Azure AI Speech service?
a: Azure AI Language handles text-based NLP tasks: analyzing written text to extract meaning, sentiment, key phrases, entities, and summaries. Its input is text (already in string form) and its output is structured NLP annotations or classifications. Core capabilities: key phrase extraction, NER, sentiment analysis, language detection, text summarization, PII detection, and custom text classification. Azure AI Speech handles audio-based capabilities: converting between speech and text, and performing real-time audio translation. Core capabilities: speech-to-text (transcribing spoken audio into written text), text-to-speech (synthesizing human-like spoken audio from text), speech translation (translating spoken audio from one language to another in real time), and speaker recognition (identifying who is speaking). The two services address different modalities: Language works on text, Speech works on audio. On AI-900 exam scenarios typically describe a business need (e.g., 'transcribe a call center recording') and ask you to pick the correct service.
fj: Azure AI Language handles all AI language tasks including voice commands — just use Language when any words are involved.
fs: Azure AI Language operates on text strings only. If the input is audio (spoken words in a .wav or .mp3 file), you need Azure AI Speech to first convert it to text. A full voice assistant pipeline typically chains Speech (audio-to-text) into Language (text NLP analysis). Confusing the two is one of the most common wrong answers on the exam.

q: What capabilities does Azure AI Speech offer, and what is the difference between speech-to-text and speech translation?
a: Azure AI Speech is a managed cloud service covering four primary capabilities. Speech-to-text (STR) transcribes spoken audio into written text in the same language — for example, converting an English call recording into an English transcript. Text-to-speech (TTS) synthesizes natural-sounding spoken audio from written text using neural voices, supporting real-time generation and batch processing of long content. Speaker recognition identifies or verifies who is speaking in an audio stream based on voice characteristics. Speech translation transcribes spoken audio in one language and simultaneously translates it into text (and optionally synthesized speech) in a different target language — for example, a Spanish speaker's words appear as English text in real time. The key distinction: speech-to-text stays in one language (transcription); speech translation converts across languages (transcription + translation). On AI-900 you need to map business scenarios to the correct capability.
fj: Speech translation and speech-to-text are the same feature — speech translation just uses a different language setting.
fs: Speech-to-text produces a transcript in the same language as the audio. Speech translation performs transcription and cross-language translation in a single pipeline — the output language differs from the input language. They use different API calls and different underlying models. A global customer support center that needs to understand French callers in English requires translation, not mere transcription.

q: What is language modeling as an NLP task, and where does it appear in Azure AI services?
a: Language modeling is the NLP task of predicting the probability of sequences of words — in other words, learning the statistical patterns of how language is used. A language model learns from vast amounts of text and develops the ability to predict what word comes next in a sequence. This foundational capability underlies almost every modern NLP and generative AI application: autocomplete, machine translation, text summarization, question answering, and text generation. In the Azure AI ecosystem, language modeling appears in two forms. Small specialized models underpin preconfigured Azure AI Language service features like sentiment analysis and NER — these are fine-tuned for narrow tasks. Large language models (LLMs) — such as GPT-4o deployed through Azure OpenAI Service — are general-purpose language models capable of generation, summarization, translation, and reasoning through natural language prompts. For AI-900 you need to know: language modeling is the core technology enabling NLP; Azure AI Language bundles preconfigured models for specific tasks; Azure OpenAI Service exposes large general-purpose language models via API.
fj: Language modeling is a specific Azure service you deploy to handle translation tasks.
fs: Language modeling is a class of techniques and models, not a single Azure service. Azure AI Language uses trained language models internally for each of its preconfigured features, and Azure OpenAI Service exposes large language models directly. Neither product is called 'Language Modeling.' The exam tests conceptual understanding: knowing that LLMs are language models scaled up to billions of parameters, not a separate service category.

q: What is Azure AI Foundry, and what is its purpose in the Azure AI ecosystem?
a: Azure AI Foundry (formerly Azure AI Studio) is Microsoft's unified platform for building, evaluating, and deploying enterprise AI applications. It serves as a central hub that brings together Azure OpenAI Service deployments, the Azure AI Foundry model catalog (hundreds of models from Microsoft, Meta, Mistral, and others), Azure AI services connections, evaluation tools, and prompt management in a single project-based workspace. A Foundry project acts as a container: it stores connection credentials to Azure resources (AI Search, Blob Storage, content safety), tracks experiment runs, and manages deployed model endpoints. The model catalog inside Foundry lets you discover, filter by task and provider, benchmark, and deploy models ranging from small open-source models to GPT-4o — all through a unified deployment API. On AI-900 you need to know: Azure AI Foundry is the portal and SDK for building production AI applications on Azure; it surfaces Azure OpenAI Service and third-party models through a single project-based interface.
fj: Azure AI Foundry is just the new name for Azure OpenAI Service — they are the same product.
fs: Azure OpenAI Service is a specific service within the Azure AI ecosystem that provides access to OpenAI's models (GPT-4, DALL-E, Whisper, Embeddings). Azure AI Foundry is a broader platform that includes Azure OpenAI Service as one capability alongside hundreds of other models, evaluation tooling, prompt flow orchestration, and connections to other Azure AI services. Foundry is the platform; Azure OpenAI Service is one of the services it surfaces.

q: What is the Azure AI Foundry model catalog, and what types of models does it include?
a: The Azure AI Foundry model catalog is a centralized repository inside Azure AI Foundry where you can discover, compare, and deploy AI models. It includes models in two broad categories. First, Microsoft Foundry Models (sold directly by Azure) include OpenAI models (GPT-4o, o3, DALL-E, Whisper, text-embedding-ada), Microsoft Phi small language models, and other first-party models. Second, models from partners and the community include hundreds of open-source and partner-contributed models from Meta (Llama), Mistral AI, Cohere, AI21 Labs, and Hugging Face. The catalog allows you to filter models by task type (text generation, embeddings, image generation, speech), benchmark results, and license. Once you select a model, you can deploy it as a managed endpoint within your Foundry project and call it through a standard REST API. On AI-900 you need to know: the model catalog is where you discover and deploy non-OpenAI models alongside OpenAI models; it contains both Microsoft and third-party models.
fj: The Azure AI Foundry model catalog only contains OpenAI models like GPT-4 and DALL-E — it is essentially a wrapper around Azure OpenAI Service.
fs: The catalog contains over a thousand models from many providers, not just OpenAI. Open-source models from Meta (Llama), Mistral, and Hugging Face community contributors are available alongside OpenAI and Microsoft's own Phi models. This breadth is the catalog's key value: it lets you evaluate and deploy best-in-class models for specific tasks without being locked to a single provider.

q: What is Azure OpenAI Service and how does it differ from OpenAI's direct API?
a: Azure OpenAI Service is Microsoft's managed deployment of OpenAI's models (GPT-4o, o3, DALL-E, Whisper, text-embedding-ada) within the Azure cloud infrastructure. It provides the same core model capabilities as OpenAI's public API but layers on Azure's enterprise features: data does not leave your Azure tenant (your prompts and completions are not used to train the base models), it integrates with Azure's identity and access management (Entra ID, RBAC, managed identities), it supports Azure's compliance certifications (HIPAA, ISO 27001, SOC 2), and it includes Microsoft's content filtering system that screens both inputs and outputs for harmful content categories (hate, sexual, violence, self-harm). Pricing is on Azure's pay-as-you-go billing, and Provisioned Throughput Units (PTUs) are available for reserved, predictable capacity. On AI-900 you need to know: Azure OpenAI Service = OpenAI models + Azure enterprise security and compliance; content filtering is built in and enabled by default.
fj: Azure OpenAI Service is identical to the OpenAI API — there is no difference, and you can use your OpenAI API key to access it.
fs: They are separate services with different authentication (Azure Entra ID vs. OpenAI API keys), different data handling commitments (Azure guarantees your data is not used for OpenAI model training), and different compliance coverage. Additionally, Azure OpenAI includes a built-in content filtering layer that OpenAI's direct API does not provide by default. For enterprise use cases involving regulated data, Azure OpenAI Service is required.

q: What is Azure OpenAI content filtering, and what four harm categories does it cover?
a: Azure OpenAI Service includes a built-in content filtering system powered by Azure AI Content Safety that automatically screens both the user's input (prompt) and the model's output (completion) for harmful content. The system classifies content into four harm categories: hate (dehumanizing content targeting groups based on identity), sexual (sexually explicit material), violence (glorifying or inciting violence), and self-harm (content encouraging or describing self-injury or suicide). Each category is evaluated at four severity levels — safe, low, medium, and high. The default configuration filters content at medium or higher severity. You can configure custom severity thresholds and create separate policies for prompts vs. completions. Content that is filtered returns an error response rather than the harmful content. On AI-900 you need to know: content filtering is enabled by default; the four categories are hate, sexual, violence, self-harm; it applies to both input prompts and output completions.
fj: Azure OpenAI content filtering only checks the model's output responses — the user's input prompt is not screened.
fs: Content filtering runs on both the input (prompt) and the output (completion). Screening inputs is critical for preventing prompt injection attacks and detecting attempts to elicit harmful content before any model compute is spent. Jailbreak detection (Prompt Shields) also operates at the prompt level. The symmetric prompt + completion filtering is a distinguishing feature of Azure OpenAI compared to using OpenAI's raw API.

q: What are the common scenarios for generative AI, and what are the responsible AI considerations specific to these workloads?
a: Generative AI models produce new content — text, images, code, audio, or video — in response to a natural language prompt or input. Common scenarios include: text generation (drafting emails, summaries, articles), code generation (writing and reviewing code), image and video generation (creating marketing visuals), question answering (chatbots that answer from a knowledge base), translation, and content moderation. Responsible AI considerations specific to generative AI include: harmful content generation (models can produce hate speech, violent content, or sexual material without guardrails — mitigated by content filtering), hallucinations (models can confidently generate false or fabricated information — mitigated by grounding in real sources and retrieval-augmented generation), intellectual property and copyright infringement (generated content may reproduce protected material), privacy risks (models may leak personal information from training data), and bias amplification (models trained on biased text can reproduce and amplify those biases). On AI-900 you need to know these risks by name and know that Azure's content filtering system addresses the harmful content risk.
fj: Generative AI applications are no different from traditional AI applications from a responsible AI standpoint — the same principles apply equally.
fs: Generative AI introduces unique risks that do not exist in traditional ML classification or regression: hallucinations (confidently wrong novel content), scale of harmful content generation, and emergent behaviors from foundation models that are harder to audit. Azure AI Foundry's content safety tooling exists specifically to address these generative-AI-specific risks, and AI-900 tests them as a distinct sub-topic under the generative AI domain.

q: What is the difference between reliability and accountability as Responsible AI principles, and how do they apply in practice?
a: Reliability and safety means an AI system behaves as expected across the full range of conditions it might encounter, including unexpected inputs and edge cases. A reliable AI system does not produce dangerous outputs when confronted with unusual but plausible scenarios, and it degrades gracefully rather than catastrophically failing. Example: an AI-powered autonomous vehicle control system that handles rare weather conditions safely rather than producing erratic steering commands. Accountability means that humans are responsible for the decisions made by or with AI systems, and that governance processes exist to review, audit, and correct AI behavior. Accountability requires that AI deployments have clear ownership, audit trails, and mechanisms for humans to override AI decisions. Example: a bank that uses AI for loan decisions must maintain human review processes and be able to explain rejected applications to regulators. The distinction: reliability is about the technical behavior of the system; accountability is about the governance structure around it. Both principles are independently testable on AI-900.
fj: Accountability and transparency are the same principle — if an AI system is transparent (explainable), then humans are automatically accountable for it.
fs: Transparency means the AI system's behavior and reasoning can be understood. Accountability means a specific human or organization is responsible for that behavior and its consequences. A transparent AI system does not automatically create accountability — you also need governance processes, clear ownership, and human override mechanisms. An opaque black-box model can still have strong accountability if the organization owns its decisions and has a review process.

q: What is Azure AI Language's entity recognition capability, and what is the difference between preconfigured NER and custom NER?
a: Named entity recognition (NER) in Azure AI Language identifies and categorizes specific real-world entities in text. The preconfigured (prebuilt) NER model recognizes standard entity types out of the box — people, locations, organizations, dates, times, monetary amounts, URLs, and more — across many languages, with no training required. You call the API with your text and receive annotated entity spans with type labels and confidence scores. Custom NER allows you to define your own entity types that are not in the prebuilt model, provide labeled training examples, and train a model to recognize those custom categories. For example, a pharmaceutical company might define 'DrugName,' 'Dosage,' and 'AdverseEffect' as custom entity types for processing clinical notes. The trade-off: prebuilt NER works immediately with no data or training cost but only covers standard entity types; custom NER requires labeled training data and training time but recognizes domain-specific entities. On AI-900 you are tested on the prebuilt capabilities; custom NER is more of an AI-102 topic.
fj: Azure AI Language's NER can only recognize the entity types it was originally trained on — you must build a completely separate custom model from scratch if you need different types.
fs: Azure AI Language offers custom NER as a built-in customization feature of the same service, not a separate product. You label examples in the Language Studio interface, train the custom model, and it is hosted and called through the same API endpoint as the prebuilt model. You do not need to build or host a separate ML pipeline.

q: What is the purpose of translation as an NLP task, and what Azure service supports it?
a: Language translation is the task of automatically converting text or speech from one natural language into another while preserving meaning. In Azure, text translation is provided by the Azure AI Translator service (part of the Azure AI Language family), which can translate text between more than 100 languages and detect the source language automatically when it is not specified. Speech translation — translating spoken audio in real time — is provided by the Azure AI Speech service, which chains speech recognition and translation into a single streaming pipeline. On AI-900 the key distinctions are: (1) text translation (written input) = Azure AI Translator, which is a distinct service from Azure AI Language's NLP capabilities; (2) speech translation (audio input) = Azure AI Speech; (3) language detection (identifying which language a text is written in) is also available through Azure AI Translator without performing full translation. Exam questions often present a scenario like 'a global e-commerce site needs to display product descriptions in 50 languages' and ask you to identify Azure AI Translator.
fj: Azure AI Language service handles all translation tasks in Azure, including both text translation and speech translation.
fs: Azure AI Language handles NLP analysis of text (sentiment, NER, key phrases). Text translation to another language is a distinct service: Azure AI Translator. Speech translation is handled by Azure AI Speech. These three services have separate API endpoints, SDKs, and pricing. Confusing Azure AI Language with Azure AI Translator is a common mistake on the exam.

q: What is model management and deployment in Azure Machine Learning, and what are the two main endpoint types?
a: After training a model in Azure Machine Learning, you register it in the Azure ML model registry, which stores versioned copies of trained model artifacts with metadata (training dataset, metrics, tags). This enables reproducibility, rollback, and promotion across environments (dev → staging → production). Deployment means packaging the model with an inference script and hosting it so applications can call it via API. Azure ML offers two endpoint types. Online endpoints serve real-time predictions: they host a model behind a REST API that responds to individual requests in milliseconds, and they support A/B testing and canary deployments by splitting traffic between model versions. Batch endpoints score large datasets asynchronously: you submit a dataset and a compute cluster processes it in parallel, writing predictions to storage — ideal for nightly scoring pipelines that do not need real-time responses. On AI-900 you need to know: online endpoints = real-time API; batch endpoints = scheduled bulk scoring. Both are managed through the Azure ML studio or SDK.
fj: Once you deploy a model as an Azure ML endpoint, you cannot update it — you must delete and redeploy a new endpoint to change the model version.
fs: Azure ML online endpoints support blue-green and canary deployments: you can add a new model version as a second deployment on the same endpoint and shift traffic to it gradually using traffic percentage settings, with no downtime. When the new version is validated, you can remove the old deployment. This is standard MLOps practice and is tested as a capability in Azure Machine Learning.

q: What are the key features of generative AI models that distinguish them from traditional ML models?
a: Generative AI models have several defining characteristics that set them apart from traditional discriminative ML models. First, they produce new content rather than predicting a category or value from inputs — they can generate text, images, code, audio, and video. Second, generative AI models are foundation models: they are pre-trained on enormous datasets at massive scale (hundreds of billions of parameters) and can be adapted to many different tasks without retraining from scratch, using only prompts or fine-tuning. Third, they use natural language as their primary interface — you interact via text prompts rather than structured feature vectors. Fourth, they exhibit emergent capabilities: behaviors that were not explicitly trained (e.g., solving arithmetic, writing code, analogical reasoning) emerge from training at scale. Fifth, they are probabilistic rather than deterministic: given the same prompt twice, a generative AI model may produce different outputs. On AI-900 you need to know these characteristics to differentiate generative AI from traditional ML in scenario questions.
fj: Generative AI models are just large versions of traditional classification models — they work the same way but with more parameters.
fs: Traditional classification models map inputs to predefined labels using discriminative boundaries. Generative AI models learn the probability distribution of the training data and can sample new examples from it. Architecturally they are different (Transformer decoder stacks vs. feedforward/tree ensembles), they are trained differently (next-token prediction or diffusion vs. label supervision), and they interact differently (free-form natural language vs. structured feature inputs).

q: What is privacy and security as a Responsible AI principle, and how does Azure implement it for AI services?
a: The Privacy and Security principle requires that AI systems protect personal data, handle sensitive information according to applicable laws and regulations, and resist adversarial attacks that could expose private information. In practice this means: not using personally identifiable information (PII) in training data without consent, implementing data minimization (only collecting what is needed), providing data subject rights (access, deletion), securing model endpoints against unauthorized access and adversarial probing, and ensuring AI-generated outputs do not leak training data. In Azure, this principle is implemented through: Azure AI Language's PII detection feature (which automatically identifies and redacts personal information from text before it is processed or stored), Azure OpenAI's commitment that customer data is not used to train base models, Entra ID (AAD) RBAC for controlling who can call AI service APIs, and Azure AI Content Safety's prompt injection detection (Prompt Shields) that guards against adversarial inputs designed to extract training data. On AI-900 you should be able to identify the privacy principle when a scenario mentions data sensitivity, regulatory compliance, or personal information in AI systems.
fj: Privacy and security in AI is primarily an IT security concern — it is handled by network firewalls and encryption, not by the AI system itself.
fs: Network security is necessary but not sufficient. AI-specific privacy risks include: the model leaking training data in its outputs, the AI service using customer inputs to retrain the model without consent, and natural language prompts that inadvertently include PII being logged and retained. These require AI-layer controls (output screening, data handling policies, prompt logging policies) in addition to standard network encryption — which is why Microsoft treats Privacy and Security as a distinct Responsible AI principle.

q: What is document processing as an AI workload, and what Azure service handles it?
a: Document processing is an AI workload that extracts structured information from semi-structured and unstructured documents such as invoices, receipts, contracts, forms, tax documents, and ID cards. It goes beyond plain OCR (which just reads text) by also identifying the meaning of text fields — for example, on an invoice it identifies which number is the invoice ID, which is the total amount, and which is the vendor name, returning them as labeled key-value pairs. Azure AI Document Intelligence (formerly Form Recognizer) is the dedicated Azure service for document processing. It includes prebuilt models for common document types (invoices, receipts, business cards, passports, tax forms) that work immediately without training, and it supports custom models that you train on your own document templates. On AI-900 you need to know: document processing workloads = structured field extraction from documents = Azure AI Document Intelligence; distinguish it from plain OCR (Azure AI Vision) which only extracts raw text.
fj: Document processing is just OCR — you use Azure AI Vision's Read API to extract text from all documents and then parse the results with your own code.
fs: Azure AI Vision's OCR Read API returns raw text in reading order — it does not understand document structure or field semantics. Azure AI Document Intelligence understands document layout and field roles: it knows that the string after 'Invoice #' is an invoice number, not just a sequence of digits. For structured field extraction from business documents, the prebuilt or custom models in Document Intelligence are the right tool, not raw OCR.
